e20bf62f-c60d-4e44-a353-b45ccf40ad3d.title: Think: Validate and reconcile extracted values from the page

details.intent: After the visit returns page content and extracted snippets, compare values from the infobox and lead/body, resolve any discrepancies, and choose the best citation for each fact.
target_facts: first_release_year
creator_name
current_stable_version
details.validation_plan: 1) For each fact, list all extracted occurrences (infobox, lead, history). 2) If multiple different values are present on the page, prefer the most prominent location (infobox) and note discrepancies. 3) Create a final validated_facts object containing chosen value + chosen citation (exact text + location on page). 4) Produce a short justification for any choice if multiple candidates exist.

measurable_output_fields.validated_facts: object { first_release_year, first_release_citation, creator_name, creator_citation, current_stable_version, stable_version_citation }

details.rationale: Ensures accuracy before persisting results; required to show the user exactly which page text justifies each reported fact.
details.parent_goal: Visit the Wikipedia page about 'Python (programming language)' at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_(programming_language) and extract the following information directly from the page content: (1) The year Python was first released, (2) The name of Python's creator, and (3) The current stable version number. You MUST visit the URL - do not rely on search results. Provide the information with citations from the actual page content.

The agent did NOT extract the three required facts (initial release year, creator name, current stable version) and marked all three as MISSING.

The merged visit content was reported as truncated and lacked the infobox/article paragraphs needed to cite the facts; the agent correctly reported this limitation and recommended re-visiting to retrieve full page content.
Because the task explicitly required extracting the facts with citations from the visited page, the agent's output does not satisfy the task requirements.

The merged visit content was reported as truncated and lacked the infobox/article paragraphs needed to cite the facts; the agent correctly reported this limitation and recommended re-visiting to retrieve full page content.
Because the task explicitly required extracting the facts with citations from the visited page, the agent's output does not satisfy the task requirements.
details.information_completeness: Incomplete. Missing the infobox and/or article paragraphs that contain the initial release year, the creator's name, and the current stable version number. Next step: re-visit the same URL and retrieve the full page content (including the infobox and relevant article text) so the exact lines or sentences for each fact can be quoted and cited.

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.

Today's featured picture
Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a 9th-century Christian
neo-Byzantine style
, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.
Photograph credit:
Diego Delso
Rece

merged_results.title: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and create final brief descriptions

result.title: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and create final brief descriptions

details.intent: From the candidate links produced by the Main Page visit, pick 10 links that are diverse and clearly describable. For each chosen link, produce: (1) exact URL as extracted, (2) link text/title, and (3) a 1-2 sentence description of what the linked page is about, indicating whether the description was derived from the Main Page context or (if Main Page lacks a usable snippet) from the linked page's first paragraph (which will be fetched in follow-up visits if required).
details.inputs_required: Output of the 'Visit Wikipedia Main Page' node (extracted_links array, context snippets, section names).
selection_criteria: Prefer links that are prominent on the Main Page (Featured article, In the news items, Did you know entries, portals) to ensure relevance to the user's request.
Ensure variety (not all links from a single section) so the final list is broadly informative.
Prefer links with an available context snippet on the Main Page; if none, mark for follow-up fetch of first paragraph.
desired_output: final_links (array of 10 objects): { url, link_text, description (1-2 sentences), description_source ('main_page' or 'linked_page_first_paragraph'), section_name }
links_needing_followup (subset of final_links that lack main-page context and therefore require visiting the linked page to produce an accurate 1-2 sentence description)
measurable_outputs: for each link: description length between ~30-300 characters, description_source set
details.verification_steps: Confirm each chosen link appears in the extracted_candidates. For any link marked 'linked_page_first_paragraph' ensure follow-up visits are scheduled to retrieve that paragraph (the execution engine should create follow-up visit nodes using the exact URLs from the Main Page output).
details.rationale: This thinking step converts raw extracted link data into a precise plan for producing the final deliverable (10 links + short descriptions). It also identifies which links already have on-page context (so no further visits needed) and which require follow-up page visits, minimizing unnecessary requests.
details.parent_goal: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.

fa2782ae-29b8-4346-8dfd-285c67567bad.title: Visit Wikipedia Main Page and extract candidate links

details.intent: Retrieve the full HTML/text of the English Wikipedia Main Page and extract a set of candidate links (URLs and link texts) and any short contextual snippets/descriptions that appear adjacent to those links on the main page.
target_facts: page_title
extracted_links (array of objects): { url, link_text, context_snippet_if_any, section_name, link_type (internal/external) }
total_extracted_links_count
details.extraction_plan: 1) Download the Main Page content. 2) Parse main content areas likely to contain notable links: mp-upper, mp-lower, Featured content (Today's featured article), In the news, Did you know, On this day, From today's featured list, portals, and other prominent blocks. 3) In each area, extract anchor tags that link to other Wikipedia pages (internal /wiki/...), capturing the exact URL (absolute), the visible link text, and up to ~200 characters of surrounding text (context snippet) where available. 4) Deduplicate links and return up to 20 candidate links (so we can choose the best 10). 5) Include the raw '_links_inline' form for each extracted URL if present so downstream visits can copy exact URLs.
measurable_output_fields: for each link: url (http/https), link_text (non-empty), context_snippet (string or null), section_name (string or null)
details.verification_steps: Check that each extracted URL is an absolute HTTP(S) URL; for internal links, ensure they start with 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/'. Confirm the Main Page HTTP status was 200 and that at least 10 internal links were extracted.
details.rationale: This visit is mandatory: the task specifically asks to 'go to the Wikipedia main page' and collect links from that page. Collecting candidate links and any on-page descriptions reduces uncertainty about which links are most appropriate to include and provides exact URLs (required for any follow-up visits).
details.parent_goal: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.
details.original_goal: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.
details.goal: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.

Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a 9th-century Christian
neo-Byzantine style
, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.

a3c5a457-32e4-4d54-8df5-fbf0b53da357.title: Select 10 links from extracted candidates and create final brief descriptions

details.intent: From the candidate links produced by the Main Page visit, pick 10 links that are diverse and clearly describable. For each chosen link, produce: (1) exact URL as extracted, (2) link text/title, and (3) a 1-2 sentence description of what the linked page is about, indicating whether the description was derived from the Main Page context or (if Main Page lacks a usable snippet) from the linked page's first paragraph (which will be fetched in follow-up visits if required).
details.inputs_required: Output of the 'Visit Wikipedia Main Page' node (extracted_links array, context snippets, section names).
selection_criteria: Prefer links that are prominent on the Main Page (Featured article, In the news items, Did you know entries, portals) to ensure relevance to the user's request.
Ensure variety (not all links from a single section) so the final list is broadly informative.
Prefer links with an available context snippet on the Main Page; if none, mark for follow-up fetch of first paragraph.
desired_output: final_links (array of 10 objects): { url, link_text, description (1-2 sentences), description_source ('main_page' or 'linked_page_first_paragraph'), section_name }
links_needing_followup (subset of final_links that lack main-page context and therefore require visiting the linked page to produce an accurate 1-2 sentence description)
measurable_outputs: for each link: description length between ~30-300 characters, description_source set
details.verification_steps: Confirm each chosen link appears in the extracted_candidates. For any link marked 'linked_page_first_paragraph' ensure follow-up visits are scheduled to retrieve that paragraph (the execution engine should create follow-up visit nodes using the exact URLs from the Main Page output).
details.rationale: This thinking step converts raw extracted link data into a precise plan for producing the final deliverable (10 links + short descriptions). It also identifies which links already have on-page context (so no further visits needed) and which require follow-up page visits, minimizing unnecessary requests.
details.parent_goal: Go to the Wikipedia main page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and collect 10 links from the page. For each link, provide: the link URL, the link text/title, and a brief description (1-2 sentences) of what the page is about. Organize the results clearly.

output.final_deliverable:
Path objective: Start at the Wikipedia page for 'Artificial intelligence' and follow links page-to-page until reaching the Wikipedia page for 'Quantum computing'. Document each visited page in order, why that link was chosen, and show how the topics connect. Evidence comes only from the provided visit results.

Numbered path (ordered URLs):
1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence
2. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science
3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing

Detailed log, reasons, and adjacency verification:

1) Page visited: "Artificial intelligence"
   - URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence
   - Visit result: success (visit action returned success: true). (Source: merged visit result titled "Visit Wikipedia: Artificial intelligence (start)")
   - Why I chose the next link: The merged results note the intent to open the 'Computer science' page as a likely intermediate linked from the AI page (merged result intent: "Open the 'Computer science' page (a likely intermediate linked from the AI page)"). That intent is the stated rationale for choosing Computer science as the next hop.
   - Evidence of outgoing links provided in the merged result: the visit returned a links_sample (truncated) and links_count (20), but the provided links_sample in the merged results does not explicitly show the Computer_science URL. Therefore: explicit evidence that the Artificial intelligence page links directly to the Computer science page is missing from the provided visit excerpt.
   - Missing evidence note: The merged result for this visit does not include an explicit occurrence of "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science" in its links_sample, so I cannot show, from the provided data, a direct link string from the AI page to the Computer science page.

2) Page visited: "Computer science"
   - URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science
   - Visit result: success (visit action returned success: true). (Source: merged visit result titled "Visit Wikipedia: Computer science (expected intermediate)")
   - Why I chose the next link: The Computer science page is a natural topical bridge from Artificial intelligence toward computational topics; the merged result explicitly lists Quantum computing in its links_sample, making it a direct candidate to reach the target.
   - Evidence that this page links to the target: the merged result for the Computer science visit includes a links_sample that explicitly contains "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing" (Source: merged visit result links_sample). Therefore we have direct evidence that the Computer science page contains a link to the Quantum computing page.
   - Adjacency verification (Computer science -> Quantum computing): Verified in provided data: Computer science page's links_sample contains the exact target URL https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing.

3) Page visited (target): "Quantum computing"
   - URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing
   - Visit result: success (visit action returned success: true). (Source: merged visit result titled "Visit Wikipedia: Quantum computing (target)")
   - Confirmation of arrival: the visit action returned the page content for the target URL, indicating the path reached the mandated target.
   - Evidence requested but missing: The merged result states the intent to "Extract lead sentence/paragraph as evidence that this is the target," but the provided visit excerpt does not include the actual lead sentence/paragraph text. Therefore the exact lead paragraph/lead sentence text is not available in the provided data.

Chain summary and explicit link chain statement:
1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence -> 2. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science -> 3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing

Adjacency/link evidence summary (explicit):
- Computer science (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science) contains a link to Quantum computing (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing). (Verified in the Computer science visit links_sample.)
- The provided visit results do NOT include an explicit link string showing that Artificial intelligence (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence) links to Computer science (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science); that specific piece of evidence is missing from the merged results.

Conclusion:
- The mandated target page (Quantum computing) was reached by visiting, in order, Artificial intelligence -> Computer science -> Quantum computing. Evidence that Computer science links to Quantum computing is present in the provided data; evidence that Artificial intelligence links to Computer science is not present in the provided visit excerpts, though the visit sequence and the merged result intent indicate that Computer science was chosen as the next page from the AI start page.

References (URLs visited, in order):
1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence
2. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science
3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing

Missing items from provided evidence (explicitly stated):
- The AI page visit excerpt does not explicitly show the Computer science URL in its links_sample; therefore a direct-string adjacency verification for AI -> Computer science is missing from the provided data.
- The Quantum computing visit excerpt does not include the page's lead sentence/paragraph text in the provided merged result; that exact text is therefore missing from the evidence.
output.action_summary: I followed this path: 1) Artificial intelligence (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence) -> 2) Computer science (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science) -> 3) Quantum computing (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing). The Computer science page's links_sample in the provided data explicitly contains the Quantum computing URL, verifying the second hop. The merged results do not include an explicit link-string proving the AI page links to Computer science, and the Quantum computing page's lead paragraph text was not included in the provided excerpts. All three pages were visited successfully per the provided visit results.

a45073a5-9c7e-4fc7-9af3-a95fd5d33389.title: Start at the Wikipedia page for 'Artificial Intelligence' (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence) and explore by following links. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Study of computation
For other uses, see
Computer science (disambiguation)
.
Fundamental areas of computer science
Programming language theory
Computer architecture
Artificial intelligence
Computational complexity theory
Computer science
History
Outline
Glossary
Category
v
t
e
Computer science
is the study of
computation
,
information
, and
automation
.
[
1
]
[
2
]
[
3
]
Included broadly in
the sciences
, computer science spans
theoretical disciplines
(such as
algorithms
,
theory of computation
, and
information theory
) to
applied disciplines
(including the design and implementation of
hardware
and
software
).
[
4
]
[
5
]
[
6
]
An expert in the field is known as a
computer scientist
.
Algorithms and
data structures
are central to computer science.
[
7
]
The theory of computation concerns abstract
models of computation
and general classes of
problems
that can be solved using them. The fields of
cryptography
and
computer security
involve studying the means for secure communication and preventing
security vulnerabilities
.
Computer graphics
and
computational geometry
address the generation of images.
Programming language theory
considers different ways to describe computational processes, and
database
theory concerns the management of repositories of data.
Human–computer interaction
investigates the interfaces through which humans and computers interact, and software engineering focuses on the design and principles behind developing software. Areas such as
operating systems
,
networks
and
embedded systems
investigate the principles and design behind
complex systems
. Computer architecture describes the construction of computer components and computer-operated equipment.
Artificial intelligence
and
machine learning
aim to synthesize goal-orientated processes such as problem-solving, decision-making, environmental adaptation,
planning
and learning found in humans and animals. Within artificial intelligence,
computer vision
aims to understand and process image and video data, while
natural language processing
aims to understand and process textual and linguistic data.
The fundamental concern of computer science is determining what can and cannot be automated.
[
2
]
[
8
]
[
3
]
[
9
]
[
10
]
The
Turing Award
is generally recognized as the highest distinction in computer science.
[
11
]
[
12
]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Computer hardware technology that uses quantum mechanics
Layered Model of Quantum Computing
Bloch sphere
representation of a qubit. The state
|
ψ
⟩
=
α
|
0
⟩
+
β
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |\psi \rangle =\alpha |0\rangle +\beta |1\rangle }
is a point on the surface of the sphere, partway between the poles,
|
0
⟩
{\displaystyle |0\rangle }
and
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |1\rangle }
.
A
quantum computer
is a (real or theoretical)
computer
that exploits
superposed
and
entangled
states
. Quantum computers can be viewed as sampling from quantum systems. These systems evolve in ways that operate on an enormous number of possibilities simultaneously, though they remain subject to strict computational constraints. By contrast, ordinary ("classical") computers operate according to
deterministic
rules. (A
classical computer
can, in principle, be replicated by a
classical mechanical device
, with only a simple multiple of time cost. On the other hand (it is believed), a quantum computer would require
exponentially
more time and energy to be simulated classically.) It is widely believed that a quantum computer could perform
some
calculations exponentially faster than any classical computer. For example, a large-scale quantum computer could
break some widely used public-key cryptographic schemes
and aid physicists in performing
physical simulations
. However, current hardware implementations of quantum computation are largely experimental and only suitable for specialized tasks.
The basic
unit of information
in quantum computing, the
qubit
(or "quantum bit"), serves the same function as the
bit
in ordinary or "classical" computing.
[
1
]
However, unlike a classical bit, which can be in one of two states (a
binary
), a qubit can exist in a
linear combination
of two states known as a
quantum superposition
. The result of
measuring
a qubit is one of the two states given by a
probabilistic rule
. If a quantum computer manipulates the qubit in a particular way,
wave interference
effects amplify the probability of the desired measurement result. The design of
quantum algorithms
involves creating procedures that allow a quantum computer to perform this amplification.
Quantum computers are not yet practical for real-world applications. Physically engineering high-quality qubits has proven to be challenging. If a physical qubit is not sufficiently
isolated
from its environment, it suffers from
quantum decoherence
, introducing
noise
into calculations. National governments have invested heavily in experimental research aimed at developing scalable qubits with longer coherence times and lower error rates. Example implementations include
superconductors
(which isolate an
electrical current
by eliminating
electrical resistance
) and
ion traps
(which confine a single
atomic particle
using
electromagnetic fields
). Researchers have claimed, and are widely believed to be correct, that certain quantum devices can outperform classical computers on narrowly defined tasks, a milestone referred to as quantum advantage or
quantum supremacy
. These tasks are not necessarily useful for real-world applications.
History
[
edit
]
For a chronological guide, see
Timeline of quantum computing and communication
.
For many years, the fields of
quantum mechanics
and
computer science
formed distinct academic communities.
[
2
]
Modern quantum theory
was developed in the 1920s to explain perplexing physical phenomena observed at atomic scales,
[
3
]
[
4
]
and
digital computers
emerged in the following decades to replace
human computers
for tedious calculations.
[
5
]
Both disciplines had practical applications during
World War II
; computers played a major role in
wartime cryptography
,
[
6
]
and quantum physics

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Computer hardware technology that uses quantum mechanics
Layered Model of Quantum Computing
Bloch sphere
representation of a qubit. The state
|
ψ
⟩
=
α
|
0
⟩
+
β
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |\psi \rangle =\alpha |0\rangle +\beta |1\rangle }
is a point on the surface of the sphere, partway between the poles,
|
0
⟩
{\displaystyle |0\rangle }
and
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |1\rangle }
.
A
quantum computer
is a (real or theoretical)
computer
that exploits
superposed
and
entangled
states
. Quantum computers can be viewed as sampling from quantum systems. These systems evolve in ways that operate on an enormous number of possibilities simultaneously, though they remain subject to strict computational constraints. By contrast, ordinary ("classical") computers operate according to
deterministic
rules. (A
classical computer
can, in principle, be replicated by a
classical mechanical device
, with only a simple multiple of time cost. On the other hand (it is believed), a quantum computer would require
exponentially
more time and energy to be simulated classically.) It is widely believed that a quantum computer could perform
some
calculations exponentially faster than any classical computer. For example, a large-scale quantum computer could
break some widely used public-key cryptographic schemes
and aid physicists in performing
physical simulations
. However, current hardware implementations of quantum computation are largely experimental and only suitable for specialized tasks.
The basic
unit of information
in quantum computing, the
qubit
(or "quantum bit"), serves the same function as the
bit
in ordinary or "classical" computing.
[
1
]
However, unlike a classical bit, which can be in one of two states (a
binary
), a qubit can exist in a
linear combination
of two states known as a
quantum superposition
. The result of
measuring
a qubit is one of the two states given by a
probabilistic rule
. If a quantum computer manipulates the qubit in a particular way,
wave interference
effects amplify the probability of the desired measurement result. The design of
quantum algorithms
involves creating procedures that allow a quantum computer to perform this amplification.
Quantum computers are not yet practical for real-world applications. Physically engineering high-quality qubits has proven to be challenging. If a physical qubit is not sufficiently
isolated
from its environment, it suffers from
quantum decoherence
, introducing
noise
into calculations. National governments have invested heavily in experimental research aimed at developing scalable qubits with longer coherence times and lower error rates. Example implementations include
superconductors
(which isolate an
electrical current
by eliminating
electrical resistance
) and
ion traps
(which confine a single
atomic particle
using
electromagnetic fields
). Researchers have claimed, and are widely believed to be correct, that certain quantum devices can outperform classical computers on narrowly defined tasks, a milestone referred to as quantum advantage or
quantum supremacy
. These tasks are not necessarily useful for real-world applications.
History
[
edit
]
For a chronological guide, see
Timeline of quantum computing and communication
.
For many years, the fields of
quantum mechanics
and
computer science
formed distinct academic communities.
[
2
]
Modern quantum theory
was developed in the 1920s to explain perplexing physical phenomena observed at atomic scales,
[
3
]
[
4
]
and
digital computers
emerged in the following decades to replace
human computers
for tedious calculations.
[
5
]
Both disciplines had practical applications during
World War II
; computers played a major role in
wartime cryptography
,
[
6
]
and quantum physics

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Study of computation
For other uses, see
Computer science (disambiguation)
.
Fundamental areas of computer science
Programming language theory
Computer architecture
Artificial intelligence
Computational complexity theory
Computer science
History
Outline
Glossary
Category
v
t
e
Computer science
is the study of
computation
,
information
, and
automation
.
[
1
]
[
2
]
[
3
]
Included broadly in
the sciences
, computer science spans
theoretical disciplines
(such as
algorithms
,
theory of computation
, and
information theory
) to
applied disciplines
(including the design and implementation of
hardware
and
software
).
[
4
]
[
5
]
[
6
]
An expert in the field is known as a
computer scientist
.
Algorithms and
data structures
are central to computer science.
[
7
]
The theory of computation concerns abstract
models of computation
and general classes of
problems
that can be solved using them. The fields of
cryptography
and
computer security
involve studying the means for secure communication and preventing
security vulnerabilities
.
Computer graphics
and
computational geometry
address the generation of images.
Programming language theory
considers different ways to describe computational processes, and
database
theory concerns the management of repositories of data.
Human–computer interaction
investigates the interfaces through which humans and computers interact, and software engineering focuses on the design and principles behind developing software. Areas such as
operating systems
,
networks
and
embedded systems
investigate the principles and design behind
complex systems
. Computer architecture describes the construction of computer components and computer-operated equipment.
Artificial intelligence
and
machine learning
aim to synthesize goal-orientated processes such as problem-solving, decision-making, environmental adaptation,
planning
and learning found in humans and animals. Within artificial intelligence,
computer vision
aims to understand and process image and video data, while
natural language processing
aims to understand and process textual and linguistic data.
The fundamental concern of computer science is determining what can and cannot be automated.
[
2
]
[
8
]
[
3
]
[
9
]
[
10
]
The
Turing Award
is generally recognized as the highest distinction in computer science.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Study of computation
For other uses, see
Computer science (disambiguation)
.
Fundamental areas of computer science
Programming language theory
Computer architecture
Artificial intelligence
Computational complexity theory
Computer science
History
Outline
Glossary
Category
v
t
e
Computer science
is the study of
computation
,
information
, and
automation
.
[
1
]
[
2
]
[
3
]
Included broadly in
the sciences
, computer science spans
theoretical disciplines
(such as
algorithms
,
theory of computation
, and
information theory
) to
applied disciplines
(including the design and implementation of
hardware
and
software
).
[
4
]
[
5
]
[
6
]
An expert in the field is known as a
computer scientist
.
Algorithms and
data structures
are central to computer science.
[
7
]
The theory of computation concerns abstract
models of computation
and general classes of
problems
that can be solved using them. The fields of
cryptography
and
computer security
involve studying the means for secure communication and preventing
security vulnerabilities
.
Computer graphics
and
computational geometry
address the generation of images.
Programming language theory
considers different ways to describe computational processes, and
database
theory concerns the management of repositories of data.
Human–computer interaction
investigates the interfaces through which humans and computers interact, and software engineering focuses on the design and principles behind developing software. Areas such as
operating systems
,
networks
and
embedded systems
investigate the principles and design behind
complex systems
. Computer architecture describes the construction of computer components and computer-operated equipment.
Artificial intelligence
and
machine learning
aim to synthesize goal-orientated processes such as problem-solving, decision-making, environmental adaptation,
planning
and learning found in humans and animals. Within artificial intelligence,
computer vision
aims to understand and process image and video data, while
natural language processing
aims to understand and process textual and linguistic data.
The fundamental concern of computer science is determining what can and cannot be automated.
[
2
]
[
8
]
[
3
]
[
9
]
[
10
]
The
Turing Award
is generally recognized as the highest distinction in computer science.
[
11
]
[
12
]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Computer hardware technology that uses quantum mechanics
Layered Model of Quantum Computing
Bloch sphere
representation of a qubit. The state
|
ψ
⟩
=
α
|
0
⟩
+
β
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |\psi \rangle =\alpha |0\rangle +\beta |1\rangle }
is a point on the surface of the sphere, partway between the poles,
|
0
⟩
{\displaystyle |0\rangle }
and
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |1\rangle }
.
A
quantum computer
is a (real or theoretical)
computer
that exploits
superposed
and
entangled
states
. Quantum computers can be viewed as sampling from quantum systems. These systems evolve in ways that operate on an enormous number of possibilities simultaneously, though they remain subject to strict computational constraints. By contrast, ordinary ("classical") computers operate according to
deterministic
rules. (A
classical computer
can, in principle, be replicated by a
classical mechanical device
, with only a simple multiple of time cost. On the other hand (it is believed), a quantum computer would require
exponentially
more time and energy to be simulated classically.) It is widely believed that a quantum computer could perform
some
calculations exponentially faster than any classical computer. For example, a large-scale quantum computer could
break some widely used public-key cryptographic schemes
and aid physicists in performing
physical simulations
. However, current hardware implementations of quantum computation are largely experimental and only suitable for specialized tasks.
The basic
unit of information
in quantum computing, the
qubit
(or "quantum bit"), serves the same function as the
bit
in ordinary or "classical" computing.
[
1
]
However, unlike a classical bit, which can be in one of two states (a
binary
), a qubit can exist in a
linear combination
of two states known as a
quantum superposition
. The result of
measuring
a qubit is one of the two states given by a
probabilistic rule
. If a quantum computer manipulates the qubit in a particular way,
wave interference
effects amplify the probability of the desired measurement result. The design of
quantum algorithms
involves creating procedures that allow a quantum computer to perform this amplification.
Quantum computers are not yet practical for real-world applications. Physically engineering high-quality qubits has proven to be challenging. If a physical qubit is not sufficiently
isolated
from its environment, it suffers from
quantum decoherence
, introducing
noise
into calculations. National governments have invested heavily in experimental research aimed at developing scalable qubits with longer coherence times and lower error rates. Example implementations include
superconductors
(which isolate an
electrical current
by eliminating
electrical resistance
) and
ion traps
(which confine a single
atomic particle
using
electromagnetic fields
). Researchers have claimed, and are widely believed to be correct, that certain quantum devices can outperform classical computers on narrowly defined tasks, a milestone referred to as quantum advantage or
quantum supremacy
. These tasks are not necessarily useful for real-world applications.
History
[
edit
]
For a chronological guide, see
Timeline of quantum computing and communication
.
For many years, the fields of
quantum mechanics
and
computer science
formed distinct academic communities.
[
2
]
Modern quantum theory
was developed in the 1920s to explain perplexing physical phenomena observed at atomic scales,
[
3
]
[
4
]
and
digital computers
emerged in the following decades to replace
human computers
for tedious calculations.
[
5
]
Both disciplines had practical applications during
World War II
; computers played a major role in
wartime cryptography
,
[
6
]
and quantum physics

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.
(

in a 9th-century Christian
neo-Byzantine style
, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.
Photograph credit:
Diego Delso
Rece

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.
(
Full article...
)

Fanny Cochrane Smith
... that recordings of the voice of

(pictured)
were used in the development of a modern Aboriginal Tasmanian constructed language?
... that
The Missing
took three years to write but only four days to film?
... that
Chicago State Cougars football
is the only
NCAA Division I
gridiron football program in Chicago?
... that only 42 votes separated the two major parties in the
2021 Liechtenstein general election
?
... that the space-combat simulation game
Renegade II
was cancelled for poor quality despite the release of demo and beta versions and reviews?
... that when finished in 1947, the
Marcel Breuer House II
was suspended from cables costing US$22?
... that
Gabriele Lösekrug-Möller
worked as a bookseller before entering politics?
... that the
1927 Salvadoran coup attempt
failed after one of its leaders was unable to convince the president that he had been overthrown?
... that Reiji Okii came up with the title of
Mr. Noone Special
in the shower?

About
Today's featured picture
Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a 9th-century Christian
neo-Byzantine style
, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.

Topics in the news
Rob Jetten
Rob Jetten
(pictured)
is sworn in as the
prime minister of the Netherlands
, leading
a minority government
.
One Battle After Another
wins
Best Film
at
the British Academy Film Awards
.
Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes
, leader of the
Jalisco New Generation Cartel
,
is killed
by the
Mexican Army
.
José Jerí
is removed
as the
president of Peru
by
Congress
, and replaced by
José María Balcázar
.

:

February 26, 2026
(
2026-02-26
)
(Thursday)
edit
history
watch
February 25, 2026
(
2026-02-25
)
(Wednesday)
edit
history
watch
Armed conflicts and attacks
2026 Cuban crisis
2026 Cuban boat incident
The
Cuban
coast guard trades fire with an
American
marked speedboat registered in
Florida
1 nautical mile (1.9 km; 1.2 mi) off of Cuba. According to
Cuban government
sources, the boat's occupants fired on coast guard personnel that approached it for identification, and injured a coast guard commander in the shootout, with returning fire killing four and injuring six unidentified gunmen.
(CBS News)
Business and economy
Economy of South Korea
South Korea
's benchmark
KOSPI
index
surpasses 6,000 points for the first time, crossing the threshold shortly after the
opening of trading
.
(AFP via BSS)
Disasters and accidents
A
Turkish Air Force
F-16
fighter jet crashes in western
Turkey
, killing the pilot.
(NOS)
International relations
Cuba–United States relations
2026 Cuban crisis
The
United States
announces that it will ease certain
restrictions
to permit
Venezuelan
oil exports
to
Cuba
's
private sector
for commercial and humanitarian purposes amid an energy shortage.
(Reuters)
India–Israel relations
Israel
's
Knesset speaker
Amir Ohana
awards
Indian
prime minister
Narendra Modi
the newly established
Medal of the Knesset
to honor him for his "significant contributions to the State of Israel and the Jewish people."
(
Times of Israel
)
Law and crime
Assassination of Marielle Franco
The
Supreme Federal Court
of
Brazil
sentences former
Rio de Janeiro
legislators
Chiquinho
and
Domingos Brazão
to 76 years and three months in prison each for ordering the 2018 killing of
city councilor
Marielle Franco
and her driver, convicting them of double aggravated homicide, participation in an armed
criminal
organization, and attempted
murder
.
(AFP via France 24)
February 24, 2026
(
2026-02-24
)
(Tuesday)
edit
history
watch
Armed conflicts and attacks
Russo-Ukrainian war
Nuclear risk during the Russo-Ukrainian war
The
Foreign Intelligence Service
of
Russia
accuses
France
and the
United Kingdom
of preparing to supply
Ukraine
with a
nuclear weapon
, prompting the
Russian foreign ministry
to warn that such a move would lead to a direct conflict between
NATO
states and Russia.
(TRT World)
(Reuters)
Myanmar Civil War (2021–present)
Myanmar Air Force
airstrikes on suspected rebel targets kill at least 17 in
Rakhine state
.
(al Jazeera)
Syrian conflict
Three
Saraya al-Jawad
gunmen and a soldier are killed in a
shootout
in
Latakia
,
Syria
.
(Al Jazeera)
Insurgency in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa
Five
police officers
are killed by the
Pakistani Taliban
during two
ambushes
in
Kohat District
,
Khyber Pakhtunkhwa
,
Pakistan
.
(AP)
Terrorism in Russia
Two people are killed, including the suspect, and two others are injured in a
suicide bombing
targeting a police car near the
Savyolovsky railway station
in
Moscow
,
Russia
.
(AFP via
Arab News
)
(Xinhua)
Four police officers are killed and three civilians injured in a suicide bombing at a checkpoint in
Bhakkar District
,
Punjab
, Pakistan.
(Xinhua)

History
is the systematic study of the
past
, focusing primarily on the
human past
. As an
academic discipline
, it analyses and interprets
evidence
to construct
narratives
about what happened and explain why it happened. Some theorists categorize history as a
social science
, while others see it as part of the
humanities
or consider it a hybrid discipline. Similar debates surround the purpose of history—for example, whether its main aim is theoretical, to uncover the
truth
, or practical, to learn lessons from the past. In a more general sense, the term
history
refers not to an academic field but to the
past
itself, times in the past, or to individual texts about the past.
Historical research
relies on
primary
and
secondary sources
to reconstruct past events and validate interpretations.
Source criticism
is used to evaluate these sources, assessing their authenticity, content, and reliability. Historians strive to integrate the perspectives of several sources to develop a coherent narrative. Different schools of thought, such as
positivism
,

merged_results.title: Post-visits: verify one adjacency pair and save chain

result.title: Post-visits: verify one adjacency pair and save chain

details.intent: After the visits complete, produce the ordered chain of URLs (at least 4). Then verify one adjacency pair by showing, from the visit outputs, the exact evidence (the _links_inline entry) proving that URL[i] links to URL[i+1]. Save the chain to memory.
target_facts: Ordered list of visited Wikipedia URLs (minimum 4) as observed from visitation results.
One verified adjacency: a snippet (link entry) from the earlier page's _links_inline showing the exact URL that points to the next page.
Indication of which node/page provided the verification snippet (node id or title).

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.
(

... that Reiji Okii came up with the title of
Mr. Noone Special
in the shower?

About
Today's featured picture
Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a 9th-century Christian
neo-Byzantine style
, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.
Photograph credit:
Diego Delso
Rece

b783fa09-ccbd-4bfa-bbae-277afa98482c.title: Visit second linked page (from Portal:Contents' links)

details.intent: From Portal:Contents, visit a commonly listed portal (Portal:Current_events) and extract its in-page links to continue the chain.
target_facts: Confirm Portal:Current_events appears in Portal:Contents' _links_inline (evidence of adjacency).
List of in-page links on https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Current_events (capture candidate links for the next hop).
Page title and one-sentence summary.
details.extraction_plan: Load page, extract _links_inline, and identify a linked Wikipedia article or portal to use as the next chain node. Record the exact URL string for the next visit.
details.verification_steps: 1) Verify this URL exists in Portal:Contents' link list. 2) From the _links_inline of Portal:Current_events, select a clear article URL for the next visit (e.g., an article linked in the 'Recent events' or navigation box).
details.rationale: Portal:Current_events is commonly linked from contents/index pages and reliably contains links to article pages. This keeps the chain living within Wikipedia and reduces ambiguity. (Vector DB auto-queried to surface link evidence when available.)
details.parent_goal: Start at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page and build a chain of at least 4 Wikipedia URLs. Each next URL must come from links found on the previous visited page. Return the chain in order, and include one verified adjacency pair near the end (show that URL[i] links to URL[i+1]).
details.original_goal: Start at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page and build a chain of at least 4 Wikipedia URLs. Each next URL must come from links found on the previous visited page. Return the chain in order, and include one verified adjacency pair near the end (show that URL[i] links to URL[i+1]).
details.goal: Start at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page and build a chain of at least 4 Wikipedia URLs. Each next URL must come from links found on the previous visited page. Armed conflicts and attacks
2026 Cuban crisis
2026 Cuban boat incident
The
Cuban
coast guard trades fire with an
American
marked speedboat registered in
Florida
1 nautical mile (1.9 km; 1.2 mi) off of Cuba. According to
Cuban government
sources, the boat's occupants fired on coast guard personnel that approached it for identification, and injured a coast guard commander in the shootout, with returning fire killing four and injuring six unidentified gunmen.
(CBS News)
Business and economy
Economy of South Korea
South Korea
's benchmark
KOSPI
index
surpasses 6,000 points for the first time, crossing the threshold shortly after the
opening of trading
.
(AFP via BSS)
Disasters and accidents
A
Turkish Air Force
F-16
fighter jet crashes in western
Turkey
, killing the pilot.
(NOS)
International relations
Cuba–United States relations
2026 Cuban crisis
The
United States
announces that it will ease certain
restrictions
to permit
Venezuelan
oil exports
to
Cuba
's
private sector
for commercial and humanitarian purposes amid an energy shortage.
(Reuters)
India–Israel relations
Israel
's
Knesset speaker
Amir Ohana
awards
Indian
prime minister
Narendra Modi
the newly established
Medal of the Knesset
to honor him for his "significant contributions to the State of Israel and the Jewish people."
(
Times of Israel
)
Law and crime
Assassination of Marielle Franco
The
Supreme Federal Court
of
Brazil
sentences former
Rio de Janeiro
legislators
Chiquinho
and
Domingos Brazão
to 76 years and three months in prison each for ordering the 2018 killing of
city councilor
Marielle Franco
and her driver, convicting them of double aggravated homicide, participation in an armed
criminal
organization, and attempted
murder
.
(AFP via France 24)
February 24, 2026
(
2026-02-24
)
(Tuesday)

history

Armed conflicts and attacks
Russo-Ukrainian war
Nuclear risk during the Russo-Ukrainian war
The
Foreign Intelligence Service
of
Russia
accuses
France
and the
United Kingdom
of preparing to supply
Ukraine
with a
nuclear weapon
, prompting the
Russian foreign ministry
to warn that such a move would lead to a direct conflict between
NATO
states and Russia.
(TRT World)
(Reuters)
Myanmar Civil War (2021–present)
Myanmar Air Force
airstrikes on suspected rebel targets kill at least 17 in
Rakhine state
.
(al Jazeera)
Syrian conflict
Three
Saraya al-Jawad
gunmen and a soldier are killed in a
shootout
in
Latakia
,
Syria
.
(Al Jazeera)
Insurgency in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa
Five
police officers
are killed by the
Pakistani Taliban
during two
ambushes
in
Kohat District
,
Khyber Pakhtunkhwa
,
Pakistan
.
(AP)
Terrorism in Russia
Two people are killed, including the suspect, and two others are injured in a
suicide bombing
targeting a police car near the
Savyolovsky railway station
in
Moscow
,
Russia
.
(AFP via
Arab News
)
(Xinhua)
Four police officers are killed and three civilians injured in a suicide bombing at a checkpoint in
Bhakkar District
,
Punjab
, Pakistan.
(Xinhua)

announces that it will ease certain
restrictions
to permit
Venezuelan
oil exports
to
Cuba
's
private sector
for commercial and humanitarian purposes amid an energy shortage.
(Reuters)
India–Israel relations
Israel
's
Knesset speaker
Amir Ohana
awards
Indian
prime minister
Narendra Modi
the newly established
Medal of the Knesset
to honor him for his "significant contributions to the State of Israel and the Jewish people."
(
Times of Israel
)
Law and crime
Assassination of Marielle Franco
The
Supreme Federal Court
of
Brazil
sentences former
Rio de Janeiro
legislators
Chiquinho
and
Domingos Brazão
to 76 years and three months in prison each for ordering the 2018 killing of
city councilor
Marielle Franco
and her driver, convicting them of double aggravated homicide, participation in an armed
criminal
organization, and attempted
murder
.
(AFP via France 24)
February 24, 2026
(
2026-02-24
)
(Tuesday)
edit
history
watch

Russo-Ukrainian war
Nuclear risk during the Russo-Ukrainian war
The
Foreign Intelligence Service
of
Russia
accuses
France
and the
United Kingdom
of preparing to supply
Ukraine
with a
nuclear weapon
, prompting the
Russian foreign ministry
to warn that such a move would lead to a direct conflict between
NATO
states and Russia.
(TRT World)
(Reuters)
Myanmar Civil War (2021–present)
Myanmar Air Force
airstrikes on suspected rebel targets kill at least 17 in
Rakhine state
.
(al Jazeera)
Syrian conflict
Three
Saraya al-Jawad
gunmen and a soldier are killed in a
shootout
in
Latakia
,
Syria
.
(Al Jazeera)
Insurgency in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa
Five
police officers
are killed by the
Pakistani Taliban
during two
ambushes
in
Kohat District
,
Khyber Pakhtunkhwa
,
Pakistan
.
(AP)
Terrorism in Russia
Two people are killed, including the suspect, and two others are injured in a
suicide bombing
targeting a police car near the
Savyolovsky railway station
in
Moscow
,
Russia
.

Arab News
)

Bhakkar District
,

, Pakistan.

Category
v
t
e
History
is the systematic study of the
past
, focusing primarily on the
human past
. As an
academic discipline
, it analyses and interprets
evidence
to construct
narratives
about what happened and explain why it happened. Some theorists categorize history as a
social science
, while others see it as part of the
humanities
or consider it a hybrid discipline. Similar debates surround the purpose of history—for example, whether its main aim is theoretical, to uncover the
truth
, or practical, to learn lessons from the past. In a more general sense, the term
history
refers not to an academic field but to the
past
itself, times in the past, or to individual texts about the past.
Historical research
relies on
primary
and
secondary sources
to reconstruct past events and validate interpretations.
Source criticism
is used to evaluate these sources, assessing their authenticity, content, and reliability. Historians strive to integrate the perspectives of several sources to develop a coherent narrative. Different schools of thought, such as
positivism
,

History
is the systematic study of the
past
, focusing primarily on the
human past
. As an
academic discipline
, it analyses and interprets
evidence
to construct
narratives
about what happened and explain why it happened. Some theorists categorize history as a
social science
, while others see it as part of the
humanities
or consider it a hybrid discipline. Similar debates surround the purpose of history—for example, whether its main aim is theoretical, to uncover the
truth
, or practical, to learn lessons from the past. In a more general sense, the term
history
refers not to an academic field but to the
past
itself, times in the past, or to individual texts about the past.
Historical research
relies on
primary
and
secondary sources
to reconstruct past events and validate interpretations.
Source criticism
is used to evaluate these sources, assessing their authenticity, content, and reliability. Historians strive to integrate the perspectives of several sources to develop a coherent narrative. Different schools of thought, such as
positivism
,

e215d718-2897-424b-8bb5-027f9c9be536.title: Post-visits: verify one adjacency pair and save chain

details.intent: After the visits complete, produce the ordered chain of URLs (at least 4). Then verify one adjacency pair by showing, from the visit outputs, the exact evidence (the _links_inline entry) proving that URL[i] links to URL[i+1]. Save the chain to memory.
target_facts: Ordered list of visited Wikipedia URLs (minimum 4) as observed from visitation results.
One verified adjacency: a snippet (link entry) from the earlier page's _links_inline showing the exact URL that points to the next page.
Indication of which node/page provided the verification snippet (node id or title).
details.extraction_plan: 1) Merge outputs from the visit nodes in order. 2) Produce an ordered chain of the selected URLs. 3) Locate, near the end of the chain, one adjacency where the parent page's _links_inline contains the exact URL of the child; include that link entry as evidence. 4) Save the final chain to memory for retrieval.
details.verification_steps: Use the visit node outputs to find the exact _links_inline entry (the tool-provided link object) that includes the child URL. Present that object (or its URL string) alongside the parent page to prove adjacency.
details.rationale: The mandate specifically asks for a verified adjacency pair; this thinking step consolidates visit outputs, extracts the required proof, and saves the result. (Vector DB auto-queried to cross-check if needed.)
details.parent_goal: Start at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page and build a chain of at least 4 Wikipedia URLs. Each next URL must come from links found on the previous visited page. Return the chain in order, and include one verified adjacency pair near the end (show that URL[i] links to URL[i+1]).
details.original_goal: Start at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page and build a chain of at least 4 Wikipedia URLs. Each next URL must come from links found on the previous visited page. Return the chain in order, and include one verified adjacency pair near the end (show that URL[i] links to URL[i+1]).
